team.name: Atlanta Falcons
team.current_draft_position: No first-round pick. First selection is 2nd round, No. 48 overall.

gm.name: Ian Cunningham
gm.title: General Manager
gm.confidence_notes: First-time GM hired January 29, 2026 — five weeks before this profile's research date. Zero drafts of his own. Profile is overwhelmingly constructed from scouting tree analysis, stated philosophy, and inference from organizations where Cunningham was a significant contributor. Must be refreshed after the 2026 NFL Draft.

career_path.role: Director of College Scouting → Asst. Director of Player Personnel → Director of Player Personnel

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Ozzie Newsome (Baltimore Ravens) — foundational evaluation principles. Secondary: Howie Roseman (Philadelphia Eagles) — roster construction and cap creativity. Tertiary: Ryan Poles (Chicago Bears) — draft capital accumulation and rebuild execution.
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Newsome/DeCosta tree → Roseman cross-pollination → Poles execution partner. The Newsome influence is the bedrock: BPA philosophy, build-through-the-draft mentality, emphasis on football players over testers, strong defense built up the middle, and accumulation of draft capital through trading down. The Roseman influence adds salary cap creativity and aggressive roster manipulation. The Poles influence adds a willingness to tear it down and rebuild methodically with an emphasis on offensive line and trading for established veterans when the price is right. Cunningham explicitly cites all three mentors by name and attributes specific lessons to each: from Newsome, 'discipline and evaluation skills'; from Roseman, 'innovation and creativity'; from Poles, 'attention to detail and leadership rooted in humility.'
scouting_tree.breakpoints: Too early to identify divergences from mentors since no decisions have been made. However, stated emphasis on 'smart, tough, physical' and 'build through the trenches' signals stronger alignment with Newsome/Ravens DNA than with Roseman's more aggressive roster churn. The explicit and repeated emphasis on draft pick accumulation is directly from the Poles/Bears playbook — and is a stated departure from predecessor Terry Fontenot's trade-up-heavy approach.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Zero drafts as GM = maximum lineage weight. The scouting tree is effectively the entire profile. After the 2026 draft, this should drop to 0.65. After two drafts, 0.40.

president_of_football.name: Matt Ryan
president_of_football.title: President of Football
president_of_football.known_biases: Former franchise QB (2008–2021), CBS Sports analyst. Has no prior front-office experience but deeply understands offensive personnel. Guided the HC and GM searches. Ryan's vision for 'smart, tough, physical' football was the foundation for hiring decisions. Ryan has stated Cunningham is 'driving the ship' on personnel. Ryan's presence in combine interview rooms with prospects (alongside Stefanski and Cunningham) indicates he will be an active participant in draft-weekend decisions, not just a figurehead.
president_of_football.notes: The NFL views Ryan as the 'primary football executive' — not Cunningham. However, Ryan himself has said Cunningham has final say on roster/personnel. This suggests Ryan sets vision/direction and Cunningham executes. The dynamic most closely resembles a team president/GM split where the president has meaningful input but the GM runs the draft board.

front_office.primary_decision_maker: Ian Cunningham (GM) — with significant input from Matt Ryan (President of Football) and Kevin Stefanski (HC)

key_influencers.name: Matt Ryan
key_influencers.title: President of Football
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded, values QB development and protection. Former MVP-level pocket passer — likely has strong opinions on quarterback evaluation. Personally recruited Stefanski and Cunningham. Connected to Ryan Poles (former Boston College teammate). Understands the value of offensive line play firsthand.

key_influencers.name: Kevin Stefanski
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded HC. Runs a wide-zone-based scheme with heavy play action and use of multiple tight ends. Kubiak/Shanahan schematic influence. Values OL athleticism and versatility, receiving TEs, and efficient pocket passers. Had input into Cunningham hiring. Bringing OC Tommy Rees and likely OL coach Bill Callahan. Stefanski's offensive scheme demands specific player types at OL, TE, and RB.

key_influencers.name: Jeff Ulbrich
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator
key_influencers.known_biases: Runs a multiple, attack-style defense that blends 3-4 and 4-3 principles. Ran significantly more man coverage with Jets (32.5%) than Atlanta had under prior staff (16.4%). Heavily emphasizes pass rush and values versatile edge rushers and linebackers. Publicly advocated for continued defensive line investment. Has already explicitly lobbied Cunningham to invest in the trenches.

key_influencers.name: Bob Quinn
key_influencers.title: Senior Advisor to the Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Lions GM (2016–2020). New England Patriots scouting tree (20 years). Brings Belichick-influenced evaluation methodology. Likely provides a secondary evaluation lens on prospects.

front_office.power_dynamics: Three-pillar structure: Matt Ryan (vision/direction), Kevin Stefanski (coaching/scheme fit), Ian Cunningham (talent evaluation/draft board/roster construction). This is a collaborative model where Ryan sets the organizational philosophy, Cunningham drives the evaluation and draft-day execution, and Stefanski has significant scheme-fit input especially on offensive players. The Falcons hired Stefanski BEFORE Cunningham, and Stefanski had 'some input' into the Cunningham hire, suggesting the HC-GM relationship will be genuinely collaborative rather than hierarchical. Ryan personally led the search process. The combine interview room includes Ryan, Stefanski, the relevant coordinator/position coach, and scouting staff — roughly 10 people total.
front_office.owner_interference: medium — Arthur Blank has fired three HCs and two GMs since 2017, restructured the entire front office (creating the President of Football role), and hired consulting firm Sportsology to evaluate football operations. Blank clearly sets organizational expectations and will move quickly if results don't materialize. However, the creation of the Ryan-Cunningham-Stefanski structure suggests Blank is delegating football decisions more than in recent years. Blank specifically recommended retaining Jeff Ulbrich, demonstrating he is still willing to influence coaching decisions.

draft_philosophy.bpa_weight_notes: Inferred from scouting tree. Ozzie Newsome's Ravens were the archetypal BPA team — 'never jumps a lower-rated player over a higher-rated player just to fill a particular hole.' Cunningham explicitly learned this: he told SI that 'drafting for need is like being a [hamster on a wheel]' — a Baltimore lesson. However, with only 5 picks and significant roster holes, the 2026 draft may be more need-driven out of necessity. Expect BPA lean to become more pronounced in future drafts as pick counts increase.
draft_philosophy.bpa_weight_by_round_notes: With only 5 picks and no first-rounder, expect a slight need-lean in 2026 out of pure necessity. BPA preference should assert itself more strongly in 2027+ as capital increases.
draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: 'Smart, tough, physical. We are going to build through the trenches. Draft, develop and retain. You can't have enough draft picks.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: Unknown — zero drafts. Stated philosophy is strongly aligned with Newsome/Ravens BPA principles and Poles/Bears draft capital accumulation. Every public statement emphasizes: (1) trench priority, (2) pick accumulation, (3) draft-develop-retain model, (4) never mortgage the future for short-term gain. The explicit contrast with Fontenot's trade-up tendencies signals a deliberate philosophical shift toward trading down.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Cannot assess stated vs. actual behavior discrepancy yet. Will be critical to evaluate after the 2026 draft whether Cunningham walks the talk on trading down and accumulating picks.

trade_behavior.trade_up_aggression_notes: Inferred. Cunningham explicitly contrasted his approach with predecessor Fontenot's, who traded up in 5 of 6 draft-day trades. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham (2022) entered with few picks and traded DOWN repeatedly to accumulate more, going from 6 to 11 picks. Cunningham's stated philosophy of 'more swings at the plate' strongly suggests he will avoid trading up except in extraordinary circumstances. Newsome's Ravens also historically preferred trading back.
trade_behavior.trade_down_willingness_notes: Very high. This is the single clearest behavioral signal from every data point available. Cunningham has said 'I love picks' multiple times, referenced the 2022 Bears turning 5 picks into 10, told his scouting staff 'this is the last year we'll ever have five picks,' and comes from three organizations (BAL, PHI, CHI) that all valued pick accumulation. The No. 48 overall pick is an ideal trade-down candidate — enough value to attract a team looking to move up for a QB or blue-chip prospect, but not so high that moving down significantly hurts pick quality.
trade_behavior.trades_per_draft_average: Unknown — first draft. Poles/Bears averaged ~3-4 trades per draft with Cunningham as AGM. Expect 2-4 trades in 2026 draft, mostly trading down.
trade_triggers: Being able to accumulate multiple Day 2/3 picks by trading back from No. 48
A team desperate for a QB or specific prospect willing to overpay to move into the top of Round 2
Clear tier break on the board where trading back 5-10 spots doesn't meaningfully reduce prospect quality
Opportunity to trade an established veteran for future draft capital (Poles/Bears model of trading Mack, Quinn, Roquan Smith)
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Hoards picks. Uses them as currency for maximum flexibility. Explicitly anti-mortgaging the future. Values current-year picks highly but won't sacrifice future picks. The Fontenot-era trade of a future 1st for James Pearce Jr. is the exact opposite of what Cunningham would do. Expect future-pick preservation to be a hard rule.

positional_value.position_value_tiers_notes: Inferred from: (1) 'Build through the trenches' stated repeatedly — EDGE, OT, DT, IOL all premium. (2) Ravens tree historically invested heavily in EDGE, OT, DT, and CB in early rounds. (3) Cunningham called WR a 'premium position' at the combine. (4) Poles/Bears invested heavily in OL (averaging 2 OL picks per draft). (5) Stefanski's scheme demands versatile OL and receiving TEs, elevating those positions. (6) Poles/Bears never drafted a RB higher than the 4th round until potentially 2025 (Jeanty at 10).
position_avoidance: RB in early rounds — Poles/Bears model deprioritized RBs in the draft, with the highest selection being a 4th-rounder. Cunningham likely shares this view, though Bijan Robinson's presence makes it moot for ATL.
position_premium: OL — 'Build through the trenches' is the mantra. Poles averaged 2 OL picks per draft. Cunningham was an OL at Virginia and understands the position intimately.
EDGE — Ulbrich's defense demands pass rushers, and the entire organizational messaging is about trench warfare.
WR — Cunningham explicitly called it a 'premium position' at the 2026 combine.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Versatile, multiple-front EDGE who can stand up or put hand in dirt. Ulbrich's defense demands positional flexibility. The Ravens tree historically valued length, power, and motor over pure speed — think Terrell Suggs (fell due to 40 time but was a dominant football player) and later picks like Matt Judon. The Poles/Bears targeted long, developing EDGEs like Austin Booker (6-4, 245) and Dominique Robinson (6-5, 253 — a raw athlete from a small school). Expect preference for big, long, versatile edge players who can rush and set the edge.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33+ inches preferred based on Ravens lineage
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Ravens historically deprioritized 40 time for EDGE (drafted Suggs with poor 40). Explosiveness (vert, broad) and change-of-direction more important than straight-line speed.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: Flexible — football speed matters more than combine speed per Newsome philosophy

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Versatility — must be able to rush from multiple alignments (3-pt, 2-pt, over tackle, over TE). Ulbrich's defense requires EDGEs who can play in 3-4 and 4-3 looks.
least_important_traits: Pure speed rushing in a straight line — scheme doesn't demand a pure speed rusher
deal_breakers: One-dimensional players who can only rush from a single alignment
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite motor and relentless effort — Ravens DNA values 'football players' above all

college_production.production_floor: Insufficient data to set firm floor. Newsome was willing to draft high-upside EDGEs with modest sack totals if the physical tools were elite.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production preferred but not required
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — scheme, usage, and competition level all factor in

evidence.tree_inference: Poles/Bears EDGE picks: Austin Booker (Rd 5, 6-4/245, developmental athlete), Dominique Robinson (Rd 5, 6-5/253, raw small-school prospect). Ravens EDGE tree: Terrell Suggs (poor 40, elite football player), Matt Judon (Rd 5 gem), Tyus Bowser (versatile stand-up). Eagles under Cunningham as Dir. of College Scouting: Derek Barnett (2017 Rd 1, productive SEC EDGE), Josh Sweat (2018 Rd 4, athletic upside play).

CB.archetype_summary: Man-coverage capable corner with physicality and ball skills. Ulbrich's defense ran man coverage 32.5% of snaps with the Jets (8th most in NFL) versus Atlanta's 16.4% under prior staff. This signals a massive shift toward man principles, demanding corners who can press and play in man. The Ravens historically valued long, physical corners (though not always tall — they drafted shorter corners with elite ball skills too). The Eagles under Cunningham's scouting tenure acquired Rasul Douglas (6-2, 209, big physical corner).

physical_profile.height_range: 5-10 to 6-2 — flexible on height if ball skills and physicality are present
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed matters but isn't an eliminator per Newsome's 'football player' philosophy. Hip fluidity and change-of-direction (3-cone, short shuttle) likely weighted more for man-coverage demands.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred, but exceptions for elite film

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Man-capable with zone versatility. Ulbrich's defense demands both but leans man significantly.
least_important_traits: Pure zone instincts in isolation — need to play man too
deal_breakers: Zone-only corners who cannot play man
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite ball skills — Ravens historically produced turnover-forcing DBs (Ed Reed, etc.)

college_production.production_floor: Unknown — Ravens valued ball production historically

evidence.tree_inference: Eagles under Cunningham scouting: Rasul Douglas (2017 Rd 3, 6-2/209, physical press corner). Poles/Bears: Tyrique Stevenson (2023 Rd 2, 6-0/198, physical man-cover corner — traded UP 5 spots to get him), Terell Smith (2023 Rd 5, physical outside corner). Ravens tree historically: Marlon Humphrey, Jimmy Smith — long, physical corners.

QB.archetype_summary: Efficient pocket passer with enough mobility to extend plays, particularly well-suited for a play-action-heavy wide-zone system. Stefanski's scheme is designed to minimize pressure on the QB through play-action, bootlegs, and quick throws off run fakes. This demands a QB who can process quickly, throw accurately on the move, and sell play action. Matt Ryan himself was the prototype Stefanski QB in terms of processing and accuracy (Ryan was an MVP pocket passer). Michael Penix Jr. (inherited) is a classic pocket passer with limited mobility but exceptional arm talent and deep ball accuracy.

physical_profile.height_range: 6-1 to 6-5 — Stefanski has worked with QBs of varying size (Baker Mayfield 6-1, Joe Flacco 6-6)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength and release speed. Mobility is a bonus, not a requirement — scheme provides layups.

playing_style.primary_style: Efficient pocket passer who excels in play-action and bootleg concepts. Must be able to process quickly, hit intermediate throws with timing, and extend plays when necessary.
most_valued_traits: Ability to throw on the move
least_important_traits: Designed rushing ability — scheme does not ask QB to run
deal_breakers: Purely run-first QBs who struggle with timing-based passing
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Not applicable — Penix is entrenched and team won't be drafting a QB early

evidence.tree_inference: Stefanski's best QB seasons were with Baker Mayfield (2020 playoffs) and Joe Flacco (2023 stretch). Both were pocket passers with adequate mobility who excelled in play-action. Poles/Bears invested the No. 1 overall pick in Caleb Williams (2024) — a mobile passer with elite arm talent and processing, suggesting the tree values dual-threat upside when available.

QB.confidence_note: Moot position for 2026 draft. Penix is the assumed starter. Ryan has not committed publicly but indicated excitement about Penix's rehab.

OT.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile offensive tackle capable of executing zone-run concepts and pass protecting in a timing-based system. Stefanski's wide-zone scheme demands OTs who can reach-block, get to the second level, and mirror in pass protection. Bill Callahan (likely OL coach, previously with Stefanski in Cleveland) develops technically sound linemen. The Poles/Bears invested heavily in OT: Darnell Wright (Rd 1, 6-5/333, athletic right tackle who made All-Rookie), Braxton Jones (Rd 5, 6-5/310, small-school developmental tackle who became a starter). Cunningham was a guard at Virginia — he understands OL evaluation at a visceral level.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 34+ inches strongly preferred based on Poles/Bears OT picks (Wright had 34.5" arms)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Lateral agility critical for zone blocking — short shuttle and 3-cone highly valued. Athletic profile matters more than pure power for zone scheme.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: N/A in traditional sense, but must be able to move laterally and reach second-level defenders

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-blocking tackle with pass protection reliability. Must be able to execute reach blocks, combo blocks, and get to linebackers at the second level.
most_valued_traits: Ability to sustain blocks through the whistle
least_important_traits: Pure power/mauling ability in a gap-scheme sense — nice to have but not primary
deal_breakers: Stiff, non-athletic tackles who can't execute zone concepts
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite movement skills — zone schemes need athletes on the line

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter expected for early-round picks
college_production.context_sensitivity: Scheme fit important — prospects from zone-blocking college systems may get a boost

evidence.tree_inference: Poles/Bears OT picks: Darnell Wright (2023 Rd 1, 6-5/333, Tennessee, athletic RT — All-Rookie), Braxton Jones (2022 Rd 5, 6-5/310, Southern Utah, small-school developmental LT — became multi-year starter), Kiran Amegadjie (2024 Rd 3, Yale, injured developmental tackle), Ozzy Trapilo (2025, Boston College). Pattern: athletic, developmental OTs who can learn technique under quality coaching.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic, versatile interior lineman who can pull, reach-block in zone concepts, and provide center-capable flexibility. Cunningham himself played guard at Virginia — this is the position group he knows best as a former player. Stefanski's scheme demands IOL who can move laterally. The Bears under Poles/Cunningham traded for Joe Thuney (All-Pro guard) and drafted Drew Dalman's development — both athletic zone-scheme fits.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Athletic testing matters — zone scheme demands mobile guards. Short shuttle and 3-cone likely weighted heavily.

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-blocking guard who can pull and get to the second level. Pass protection anchoring important but athleticism prioritized.
most_valued_traits: Ability to pull and lead on zone runs
least_important_traits: Pure anchor strength in isolation — scheme doesn't ask for telephone-booth pass protection

evidence.tree_inference: Bears acquired Drew Dalman (trade), Joe Thuney (FA) — both zone-scheme athletic IOL. Poles/Bears drafted Luke Newman (2025 OL). Ravens historically valued Marshal Yanda — one of the greatest guards ever, equally adept in zone and power. Note: Falcons already have Chris Lindstrom (elite RG, Pro Bowler) and traded Drew Dalman to Bears. Center and LG likely higher priorities.

DT.archetype_summary: Penetrating, athletic interior defensive lineman. Ulbrich's defense produced a franchise-record 57 sacks in 2025, and he has publicly advocated for continued DL investment. Newsome's philosophy was 'a strong defense is built up the middle' — the Ravens drafted Haloti Ngata (5x Pro Bowl DT) to anchor the interior. Poles/Bears invested in DT: Gervon Dexter Sr. (2023 Rd 2, athletic 3-tech), Zacch Pickens (2023 Rd 3). The Falcons need interior help — Orhorhoro has been a 'disappointment' and Onyemata is aging.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Upfield quickness and first-step explosion. Athletic DTs who can penetrate are preferred based on both Ravens and Bears trees.

playing_style.primary_style: Penetrating interior disruptor for Ulbrich's attack-style defense. Must be able to generate interior pressure and collapse the pocket.
most_valued_traits: Ability to two-gap and penetrate based on call
least_important_traits: Pure run-stuffing nose tackle play in isolation — Ulbrich wants DTs who can rush the passer too
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite interior pass rush — Ulbrich's defense demands it

evidence.tree_inference: Ravens tree: Haloti Ngata (2006 Rd 1, athletic DT who could rush the passer). Bears/Poles tree: Gervon Dexter Sr. (2023 Rd 2, 6-3/310, athletic 3-tech from Florida — became starting 3-tech). Ulbrich at Jets ran a front that generated interior pressure; Quinnen Williams thrived under him.

WR.archetype_summary: Cunningham called WR a 'premium position' at the 2026 combine, and it's a clear team need (WR2 opposite Drake London). Stefanski's offense features lots of in-breaking and crossing routes per Next Gen Stats data from Cleveland, plus play-action deep shots. This demands route runners who can win on crossers, posts, and intermediate breaks. The Eagles under Cunningham's scouting tenure produced a WR-friendly environment. Poles/Bears drafted Rome Odunze (2024 Rd 1, 6-3/212, complete outside WR), Luther Burden III (2025 Rd 2, versatile slot/outside), and Tyler Scott (Rd 4, deep threat).

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Route running ability matters more than straight-line speed for Stefanski's timing-based passing. However, speed helps for play-action deep shots.

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile route runner who can win on intermediate and crossing routes in Stefanski's system. Strong hands and ability to win contested catches complement Drake London's skill set.
most_valued_traits: Ability to win on in-breaking routes
Versatility to play outside and slot
least_important_traits: Pure deep-threat vertical speed in isolation — scheme creates deep opportunities through play action, not just raw speed
deal_breakers: Route-running limitations that would undermine timing-based passing
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Separation ability and route polish — the hallmark of a Stefanski-system WR

evidence.tree_inference: Poles/Bears WR picks: Rome Odunze (2024 Rd 1, 6-3/212, complete outside WR, FBS-leading 1,640 yards), Luther Burden III (2025 Rd 2, versatile). Stefanski's best WR work was with Amari Cooper (elite route runner) in Cleveland. Eagles under Cunningham: drafted Dallas Goedert (TE, but highlights receiving focus), Jordan Mailata (OL). WR is a clear 2026 draft target.

TE.archetype_summary: Receiving-capable TE who can also block — a Stefanski system staple. Multi-TE sets are a core feature of Stefanski's offense. The Ravens historically invested heavily in TE (Newsome was a HOF TE himself — he drafted Hayden Hurst and Mark Andrews in the same 2018 draft). Poles/Bears drafted Colston Loveland (2025 Rd 1 range, elite receiving TE from Michigan). With Kyle Pitts franchise-tagged, the Falcons need a TE2 who can block and catch in Stefanski's multi-TE looks.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Receiving athleticism and blocking ability both matter. Hybrid profile preferred.

playing_style.primary_style: Hybrid TE who can line up inline, in the slot, or flexed out. Must block credibly in the run game AND be a legitimate receiving threat.
most_valued_traits: Ability to find soft spots in zones
least_important_traits: Pure blocking TE with no receiving threat — Stefanski needs multiple pass-catching TEs
deal_breakers: Cannot block at all — must contribute to the run game in Stefanski's zone scheme

evidence.tree_inference: Newsome/Ravens: Mark Andrews (2018 Rd 3, elite receiving TE), Hayden Hurst (2018 Rd 1, athletic hybrid). Poles/Bears: Colston Loveland (2025, elite receiving TE from Michigan). Stefanski's offense in Cleveland heavily featured David Njoku. The Falcoholic projects ATL could target BYU's Carsen Ryan (6-4/250, run-blocking TE with receiving upside) in later rounds.

S.archetype_summary: Versatile, rangy safety who can play single-high and in the box. Ulbrich's defense with the Jets featured a lot of single-high looks (Cover 1 was 7th most used in NFL at 28.6%). This demands a true centerfield safety with range. The Ravens historically produced HOF safety Ed Reed (the ultimate rangy FS) and continued to invest in the position (Malaki Starks, 2025 Rd 1). The Falcons have Jessie Bates III (elite FS under contract) and Xavier Watts (2025 rookie) — safety is not a top need.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Range is paramount — deep speed and ability to cover ground in single-high.

playing_style.primary_style: Rangy centerfield safety for Cover 1/Cover 3 looks. Must be able to play the deep middle and also come down to support the run.
least_important_traits: Box safety/run support ability in isolation — scheme needs coverage first

evidence.tree_inference: Ravens: Ed Reed (HOF), Malaki Starks (2025 Rd 1). Bears/Poles: Jaquan Brisker (2022 Rd 2, 6-1/200, versatile safety from Penn State). Ulbrich's Jets featured hybrid safety usage with Sauce Gardner and DJ Reed at corner, allowing safety flexibility.

LB.archetype_summary: Versatile, athletic linebacker who can cover and blitz — the modern multi-tool LB. Ulbrich was a former NFL linebacker himself and values the position. Ulbrich's defense with the Jets used LBs like CJ Mosley and Quincy Williams in coverage and as blitzers. The Falcons had Kaden Elliss produce an excellent 2025 under Ulbrich (2nd among LBs with 30 pressures), suggesting Ulbrich values LBs who can rush the passer. Ravens tree historically valued elite LBs: Ray Lewis (GOAT), CJ Mosley. The Bears under Poles primarily addressed LB through FA/trade (Roquan Smith was traded away, Tremaine Edmunds signed).

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed and coverage ability — sideline-to-sideline range. Must be able to cover TEs and RBs in man.

playing_style.primary_style: Three-down linebacker who can cover, blitz, and play the run. Ulbrich's system demands LBs who are scheme-versatile.
least_important_traits: Pure downhill thumper play — modern scheme needs coverage first
deal_breakers: Coverage liabilities — Ulbrich's defense asks LBs to cover in space

evidence.tree_inference: Ravens: Ray Lewis, CJ Mosley (elite all-around LBs). Falcons 2025 draft: Jalon Walker (Rd 1, pick 15, Georgia — versatile LB/EDGE hybrid who had 5.5 sacks as a rookie). Ulbrich's system at Jets used CJ Mosley and Quincy Williams. Elliss produced 30 pressures in 2025 — Ulbrich values blitzing LBs.

RB.archetype_summary: Complementary/backup RB behind Bijan Robinson. Stefanski's scheme features a heavy commitment to the run (wide zone) and has historically used a lead back heavily but needs capable depth. With Robinson as the clear RB1, the Falcons need a change-of-pace/pass-catching backup. Poles/Bears drafted RBs only in mid-to-late rounds (Roschon Johnson Rd 4, Trestan Ebner Rd 6) and signed D'Andre Swift in FA. The Falcons' Tyler Allgeier may depart in FA.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed, vision, and receiving ability. Zone scheme demands cut-back ability and patience.

playing_style.primary_style: Complementary back who can spell Robinson and contribute as a pass catcher. Zone-scheme fit with vision and cut-back ability.
deal_breakers: Pure power back with no pass-catching utility

evidence.tree_inference: Poles/Bears: Roschon Johnson (Rd 4, power/short-yardage), Trestan Ebner (Rd 6, speed/receiving). Stefanski used Nick Chubb (power zone runner) and later added complementary backs in Cleveland. RB is a Day 3 or UDFA priority for ATL, not a premium draft investment.

reach_tolerance.notes: Scouting tree suggests moderate-to-low reach tolerance. Newsome's Ravens were known for staying disciplined to their board. Poles/Bears similarly avoided reaches in early rounds, preferring to trade down if value wasn't available. Expect Cunningham to stick closely to consensus value rather than reaching significantly.

scheme_fit.scheme_weight_notes: Moderately high scheme weight. Stefanski's wide-zone offense demands specific OL traits (athleticism over pure power), TE versatility, and efficient pocket passing. Ulbrich's defense demands versatile front-seven players and man-capable corners. Both coordinators are retained and bringing established systems, which means scheme fit will be a significant factor in 2026 draft picks.
scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Stefanski system: Wide-zone run scheme foundation with heavy play-action passing. Multiple tight end sets. Bootlegs and crossing routes. Designed to minimize QB pressure through scheme. OL coach likely Bill Callahan (elite zone-run OL developer). OC Tommy Rees (followed Stefanski from Cleveland). Expects athletic OL, versatile TEs, route-running WRs, and an efficient pocket passer.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Ulbrich system: Multiple front, attack-style defense blending 3-4 and 4-3 principles. Significantly more man coverage than previous ATL staffs (32.5% man rate with Jets vs. 16.4% in ATL previously). Cover 1 emphasis. Aggressive pass rush philosophy (57 sacks in 2025, franchise record). Demands versatile EDGEs, penetrating DTs, coverage-capable LBs, and man-press corners.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: COMPLETE REGIME CHANGE. New GM (Cunningham), new HC (Stefanski), new OC (Rees). DC Ulbrich retained for Year 2 continuity on defense. Offense is switching from the Zac Robinson/Morris system to Stefanski's wide-zone/play-action system. Major schematic shift on offense; defensive scheme building on Ulbrich's Year 1 foundation. Confidence in scheme-based projections is MEDIUM for defense (Ulbrich Year 2) and LOW for offense (new installation).

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderately conservative, inferred from scouting tree. Newsome's Ravens were known for avoiding character risks (passed on Laremy Tunsil due to off-field concerns in 2016). The Poles/Bears avoided Jalen Carter (character concerns) by trading back one spot in 2023. However, Roseman/Eagles are more willing to take risks, creating a potential tension in Cunningham's calculus. Net: Expect a generally conservative approach with the occasional calculated risk, particularly on Day 3.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Low-to-moderate. As a first-time GM, Cunningham may be particularly conscious of looking at the full picture rather than overreacting to one season's results. However, the organizational pressure from 8 consecutive losing seasons could create urgency to address needs over BPA. Expect him to resist recency bias intellectually but face real organizational pressure to 'fix' visible problems.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Too early to assess. Bears/Poles model was to quickly move on from failed acquisitions (cut Chase Claypool after one disappointing half-season). Expect Cunningham to follow suit — unlikely to double down on failed investments.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Balanced-to-film-first, inferred from scouting tree. Newsome 'never got too carried away with height, weight or speed — he wanted football players.' The Ravens drafted Terrell Suggs despite a poor 40 time because the film was elite. This suggests Cunningham will be film-first but use measurables as confirming data, not primary data. Cunningham's background as a player (he understands the physical demands firsthand) may make him slightly more measured-aware than a pure scout.
psychological_profile.character_description: Moderately high character standards, inferred from tree. Newsome's two most-asked questions were 'How will he fit in our locker room?' and 'Does he help us beat the Steelers?' The Poles/Bears avoided Jalen Carter on character grounds. However, the Eagles under Roseman have been more willing to take character risks. Net: expect a moderate character bar — not as strict as Newsome, but not as loose as some teams. Worth noting: the Falcons have a current situation with James Pearce Jr. (domestic violence arrest) that will be an early test of character tolerance.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Build around the franchise QB, protect him, and surround him with weapons. The entire Stefanski offensive philosophy (wide-zone, play-action, TE-heavy) is designed to make the QB's life easier. Ryan (as President) has an obvious deep understanding of QB play. Penix Jr. is the incumbent despite an ACL tear. Cunningham/Ryan will not publicly guarantee Penix but have expressed excitement about his rehab. Unlikely to draft a QB in 2026 — will add a veteran backup instead.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Balanced, leaning slightly toward production. Newsome valued football players who produced, but was also willing to bet on traits (drafted raw athletes like Terrell Suggs who were productive but had testing concerns). Poles/Bears showed a willingness to take developmental athletes on Day 3 (Dominique Robinson, Austin Booker from small schools) while drafting producers early (Darnell Wright was highly productive at Tennessee, Rome Odunze led FBS in receiving yards). Net: production matters, especially in early rounds, but projection picks happen on Day 3.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: No data — too early to identify thresholds
production_thresholds_by_position.OL: Multi-year starter expected for Day 2 picks based on Bears patterns
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No data — film likely matters more than stats per Ravens lineage

college_production_preferences.notes: Insufficient data to set firm thresholds. The scouting tree suggests balanced weighting — Ravens valued productive college players but didn't use statistical thresholds as hard cutoffs. The Eagles under Roseman/Cunningham were similarly balanced.

CB.notes: Man-coverage demands speed. Sub-4.50 likely preferred. Hip fluidity and change-of-direction critical.

EDGE.notes: Length matters — 33+ inch arms based on Ravens lineage. Speed is secondary to explosiveness and football speed per Newsome philosophy.

OT.notes: Athletic testing matters for zone scheme. 34+ inch arms likely minimum. Lateral agility (short shuttle) critical.

WR.notes: Route running over raw speed. No clear size or speed threshold — Poles/Bears drafted WRs across the athletic spectrum.

DT.notes: First-step quickness and explosiveness valued over pure size. Must be able to penetrate.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Mostly soft preferences — Newsome philosophy explicitly rejected hard measurable cutoffs ('never got too carried away with height, weight or speed').

size_preferences_by_position.DT: Athletic interior lineman, 280+ for 3-tech, 310+ for nose
size_preferences_by_position.EDGE: Length and versatility over pure size
size_preferences_by_position.LB: Flexible — need to be fast enough to cover

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: Explosiveness indicators (vertical jump, broad jump) likely valued more than 40 time based on Ravens tree. 3-cone and short shuttle critical for EDGE, CB, and OL in zone-scheme evaluation.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first. This is directly from Newsome: 'He wanted football players. Period.' The Ravens famously drafted Terrell Suggs despite a poor 40 because the film showed an elite pass rusher. Expect Cunningham to share this film-first approach while using measurables as supporting evidence.

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: N/A — No first-round pick in 2026 (traded to Rams by Fontenot for James Pearce Jr.). This is the defining constraint of Cunningham's first draft.
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: This is where Cunningham's draft starts. Pick 48 (Rd 2) and Pick 79 (Rd 3) are his premium selections. STRONG expectation that he will try to trade down from 48 to accumulate more picks, potentially splitting it into two late-2nd/early-3rd selections or a 3rd + Day 3 picks. If he stays at 48, expect a BPA selection at a premium position (EDGE, CB, WR, DT, OL). Round 3 at 79 will likely address a different need. The Poles/Bears model showed willingness to both trade up in Round 2 for targeted players (Tyrique Stevenson) and trade down in Rounds 3-4 for additional picks.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes and depth. Poles/Bears found gems in Round 5 (Braxton Jones, Dominique Robinson) by targeting athletes from smaller programs. Expect Cunningham to target at least one developmental OL, one depth defender, and one special teams contributor on Day 3. Will likely try to manufacture additional Day 3 picks through trades.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: N/A — Falcons have no compensatory picks in 2026. However, Cunningham will likely be aware of compensatory formula implications when making FA decisions, trying to set up comp picks for 2027.

hit_rate_analysis.hit_rate_by_position: N/A — no draft history as GM
hit_rate_analysis.blind_spots: Unknown — will need to evaluate after 2-3 drafts
hit_rate_analysis.strengths: College scouting background suggests strong prospect evaluation foundation. Was Director of College Scouting for Eagles during a period when the team drafted well (Derek Barnett, Dallas Goedert, Josh Sweat, Jordan Mailata). Eagles under his scouting tenure produced multiple Pro Bowlers.
hit_rate_analysis.notes: The Eagles players drafted during Cunningham's tenure as Director of College Scouting (2017-2018) provide the closest approximation of his personal evaluation preferences, though he didn't have final say. Key names: Derek Barnett (EDGE, Rd 1), Sidney Jones (CB, Rd 2), Rasul Douglas (CB, Rd 3), Dallas Goedert (TE, Rd 2), Josh Sweat (EDGE, Rd 4), Jordan Mailata (OT, Rd 7 developmental).

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Moderate-to-conservative FA spender who uses the draft as the primary roster-building tool. 'Draft, develop, retain' is the explicit philosophy. Newsome's Ravens were famous for patience in FA ('right player, right price'). Poles/Bears rebuilt primarily through the draft with targeted veteran additions (Montez Sweat trade, Keenan Allen trade). Cunningham explicitly said he never wants to 'mortgage the future for the now.' Expect strategic FA signings to fill 1-2 starting-caliber holes, with the draft doing the heavy lifting.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a FA signing fills a position, expect draft priority at that position to decrease but not disappear — depth-building is a stated priority. Cunningham specifically noted wanting to avoid a 'top-heavy' roster.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft. Supplement with selective FA. Retain homegrown talent. Use cap creativity (learned from Roseman in Philadelphia) to create flexibility. Explicitly anti-dead-money. The Falcons are releasing Kirk Cousins ($180M contract, Fontenot-era signing) as one of Cunningham's first moves — demonstrating willingness to cut ties with expensive mistakes quickly.

notable_patterns: ZERO DRAFTS AS GM — all patterns are inferred from scouting tree

Cunningham's closest direct influence on draft picks was as Director of College Scouting for Eagles (2017-2018): Derek Barnett (EDGE, Rd 1), Sidney Jones (CB, Rd 2), Rasul Douglas (CB, Rd 3), Dallas Goedert (TE, Rd 2), Josh Sweat (EDGE, Rd 4), Jordan Mailata (OT, Rd 7)

As Bears AGM (2022-2025), contributed to drafts that selected: Kyler Gordon (CB, Rd 2), Jaquan Brisker (S, Rd 2), Darnell Wright (OT, Rd 1), Gervon Dexter (DT, Rd 2), Tyrique Stevenson (CB, Rd 2), Caleb Williams (QB, Rd 1), Rome Odunze (WR, Rd 1), Colston Loveland (TE), Luther Burden III (WR)
Pattern across both trees: heavy investment in OL, EDGE, CB, and DT in early rounds. WR and TE also valued. RB deprioritized.

2026_draft_capital.notes: Extremely limited capital. No 1st-rounder (traded to Rams by Fontenot). No 5th-rounder. Cunningham has explicitly said he wants to manufacture more picks and expects to end with more than 5. Expect trades — likely trading down from 48 and/or making roster moves to acquire picks.

primary_needs: WR2 — Cunningham called WR a 'premium position'; Mooney's future uncertain, need a complement to Drake London
CB2 — Need a starter opposite A.J. Terrell, especially one who can play man in Ulbrich's scheme
DT — Interior defensive line depth needed; Onyemata aging, Orhorhoro hasn't developed as hoped
EDGE depth — Pearce Jr.'s legal situation creates uncertainty; Ulbrich wants continued DL investment
secondary_needs: OT — Jake Matthews turning 34, Kaleb McGary missed 2025 with injury; succession planning needed
LB — Kaden Elliss is a free agent; need to address ILB
TE2 — Need a blocking TE to complement Pitts in Stefanski's multi-TE sets
RB2 — Tyler Allgeier likely departing; need backup/complement to Bijan Robinson
non_needs: QB — Michael Penix Jr. expected back from ACL; will add veteran backup in FA, not draft
S — Jessie Bates III and Xavier Watts provide strong safety duo
WR1 — Drake London is a cornerstone
RB1 — Bijan Robinson is a franchise star

confidence_notes: This is a brand-new regime with zero drafts together. GM Ian Cunningham was hired January 29, 2026 — five weeks before this research date. HC Kevin Stefanski was hired January 17, 2026. President of Football Matt Ryan was hired January 10, 2026. Every behavioral projection in this profile is inferred from: (1) Cunningham's scouting tree under Newsome, Roseman, and Poles (0.85 weight); (2) Cunningham's stated philosophy in press conferences and combine media sessions (0.10 weight); (3) Scheme demands from Stefanski (offense) and Ulbrich (defense) (0.05 weight). THIS PROFILE MUST BE SUBSTANTIALLY REVISED AFTER THE 2026 NFL DRAFT, when actual draft behavior can be analyzed.
